Title: Engineering the production of conjugated fatty acids in Arabidopsis thaliana leaves

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Eleostearic acid (ESA)-containing neutral lipids / leaf oil (TAG)
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves, neutral lipid/TAG fraction, lipid droplets
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of PXA1 increases steady-state neutral lipid accumulation and enhances ESA accumulation in leaves when FADX and DGAT2 are co-expressed, but also increases ESA-associated cytotoxicity and growth defects.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. pxa1 mutant background showed elevated neutral lipid content due to impaired fatty acid breakdown. 2. In mature leaves co-expressing tung FADX and DGAT2, pxa1 further increased leaf neutral lipid accumulation relative to WT. 3. pxa1 plants were more sensitive to ESA production, showing stronger yellowing/necrosis, higher ion leakage, and induction of senescence-associated markers.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; transgenic co-expression with tung FADX and DGAT2; GC/FID lipid profiling; confocal microscopy; electrolyte leakage assay; qRT-PCR of ACX4 and LPCAT1
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf developmental stage (15-day seedlings, 42-day mature leaves); soil growth; sucrose supplied for pxa1 seedling establishment
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Industrial feedstock and nutritional end-use fatty acid; contributes to high-energy leaf oil
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PXA1 also transports lipid hormone precursors into peroxisomes for beta-oxidation, so disruption causes pleiotropic effects on growth, development, stress response, and seedling establishment.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Requires exogenous sugar for seedling establishment; compromised growth/development and stress response; stronger leaf yellowing, necrosis, and ion leakage when ESA is produced.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can elevate unusual fatty acid-containing leaf oil by blocking degradation, but deployment likely requires inducible or tissue-specific silencing to avoid establishment and growth penalties.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Eleostearic acid (ESA)-containing neutral lipids / leaf oil (TAG)
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: cgi-58 background supported ESA engineering, but effects on neutral lipid and ESA accumulation were weaker than in WT or pxa1, likely because transgene expression was relatively low.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. cgi-58 mutants were included as high-leaf-oil backgrounds with disrupted fatty acid breakdown. 2. Co-expression phenotypes in cgi-58 were less pronounced than in WT or pxa1. 3. Reduced performance was attributed by the authors mainly to lower FADX and DGAT2 transgene expression in this background.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant background comparison; transgenic co-expression with tung genes; GC/FID lipid profiling; qRT-PCR; confocal microscopy
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Leaf developmental stage (15-day seedlings, 42-day mature leaves)
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Industrial feedstock and nutritional end-use fatty acid; contributes to high-energy leaf oil
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: cgi-58 is part of the engineered background rather than the heterologous production module; not a direct ESA biosynthetic enzyme.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential supporting background for high-leaf-oil engineering, but weaker performance and lower transgene expression reduce utility in this study.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Col-0 WT; pxa1 mutant; cgi-58 mutant
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Modification of phosphatidylcholine-linked linoleate/oleate in ER glycerolipid metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Phosphatidylcholine-linked unsaturated C18 fatty acid, primarily linoleic acid-derived acyl chains
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces a conjugated triene structure into ER phospholipid-linked fatty acid substrate to produce eleostearic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key determinant of ESA production; establishes the unusual fatty acid but alone is insufficient for efficient accumulation in TAG
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Industrial and nutritional unusual fatty acid; potential bio-based feedstock and possible anti-pest function
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf neutral lipids/TAG and trace amounts in polar lipids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression of tung FADX enables ESA production in Arabidopsis leaves, but ESA remains low when FADX is expressed alone and causes growth defects; co-expression with DGAT2 greatly increases ESA accumulation and improves plant performance.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FADX is the enzyme responsible for ESA synthesis in engineered Arabidopsis leaves. 2. FADX alone produced only low ESA accumulation, with trace ESA in polar lipids and about 2% ESA in neutral lipids. 3. Co-expression with tung DGAT2 increased ESA in neutral lipids to about 12% and substantially increased total leaf oil. 4. FADX expression alone caused stunting, yellowing, and elevated ion leakage, consistent with ESA-associated cytotoxicity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Constitutive transgenic expression; GC/FID fatty acid profiling of neutral/polar lipids; TLC fractionation of phospholipids and galactolipids; qRT-PCR; confocal microscopy; phenotyping and electrolyte leakage assay
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Seed-expressed in tung as inferred from tung seed oil ESA production
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Ectopically expressed in 15-day seedlings and mature leaves via constitutive promoter
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive expression in leaves; analyses at seedling and mature leaf stages
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper discusses possible inhibition of endogenous FAD2 activity and strong exclusion of ESA from phospholipids; majority of ESA appears to be synthesized in ER phospholipids and then removed from membranes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: FADX alone is detrimental to growth, causing leaf yellowing, necrosis, and higher ion leakage.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential catalytic module for engineering ESA production in vegetative tissues; should be paired with selective TAG-assembly enzymes such as same-species DGAT2 to improve flux and reduce toxicity.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: Col-0 WT; pxa1 mutant; cgi-58 mutant
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Diacylglycerol plus acyl-CoA, including ESA-containing acyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acylates diacylglycerol with acyl-CoA to form TAG, effectively capturing and sequestering ESA into neutral lipids.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for storage/sequestration of target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key channeling/storage step for unusual fatty acid accumulation in TAG
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Improves storage of industrially valuable ESA in neutral lipids and increases leaf oil energy density
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DGAT2 alone increases leaf neutral lipid content, and when co-expressed with FADX causes a synergistic increase in total leaf oil and strongly enhances partitioning of ESA from polar lipids into TAG.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DGAT2 expression alone approximately doubled neutral lipid content in leaves. 2. Co-expression with FADX synergistically increased total neutral lipids and ESA accumulation. 3. DGAT2 reduced ESA-associated growth defects, consistent with sequestration of toxic ESA metabolites into TAG. 4. In pxa1 background, DGAT2 plus FADX further increased mature-leaf neutral lipids though plant health worsened because of impaired degradation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Constitutive transgenic expression; GC/FID lipid profiling; confocal microscopy of lipid droplets; qRT-PCR; plant phenotype and electrolyte leakage assays
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Seed-associated in tung oil biosynthesis context
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Ectopically expressed in seedlings and leaves via constitutive promoter
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive expression in leaves; tested in WT, pxa1, and cgi-58 backgrounds
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: dual CaMV 35S promoter/terminator in binary plasmid B9
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors interpret DGAT2 as selectively channeling ESA after its removal from phospholipids; more effective than endogenous leaf acyltransferases for ESA incorporation into TAG.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Generally improves plant growth relative to FADX alone, but in pxa1 background cannot fully suppress ESA-related damage.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for stacking with unusual fatty-acid-producing enzymes from the same source species to maximize TAG sequestration and reduce toxicity.
